speaker: interviewee
text: After--

speaker: interviewer
text: Take number four.

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon? Take number? Oh. At Buchenwald, um, this-- this driver said you've got to see this. And he took me to the crematorium, which he-- he said-- And there were four ovens, and they-- and they were cold, but there were bodies in each of the crematorium ovens. And he said, what he told me, now I didn't see this. When they liberated the camp, they were cremating bodies, and they couldn't figure out how to turn these things off. They wanted to turn them off. And next to the crematoriums was a room filled with corpses, like the pictures of the skeletal corpses. Outside in the open were bodies stacked six, eight, feet tall. And this is where I-- you've heard the coin-- the phrase, cor-- stacked like cord wood. That's what they were stacked like. Nice even stacks. The skin looked like it was tanned, like-- almost like leather. I touched a couple of-- uh-- and I mean I was not too squeamish, but I didn't want to do it. But he said t-- the skin is just like leather, like upholstery on a chair. And that's what it felt like. And it-- it had a very distinct color to it. It didn't look like white skin. Uh, but there was-- And the other thing, the smell-- of the smell, many of these people had died of dysentery, starvation. Their bowels had moved. They had been in-- in an environment where they were helpless. Now this is hard. [PAUSES] And they-- they couldn't get away from each other. I mean, they were incapacitated, and they were-- they were having bowel movements, stools-- loose stools, on-- on each other. And even if in their beds, if they happened to be in the beds, there was just uh, stool, fecal matter, just all over. Like that-- the-- the combination of that smell plus this death smell was what was the hardest thing for me to cope with, sense wise. The sensory data, what you could see, was just too much. Um, it was almost like it wasn't real. It was surreal. You know, I mean, you could see these dead people, and there were hundreds of them, hundreds of them, in this nice-- in these stacks. You could walk down a half football field length, on both sides would be these-- and the smell was there, of course, all the time. And uh-- [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewer
text: What about people that were still alive? What contact did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that-- they-- the people that were still alive, were-- by the time I got there, were being cared for by the medics from the army. And 50% of them were lost. I mean, they were-- they were-- they were trying to-- First, first they were giving them penicillin, which of course was the cure-all for everything in those days, and sulfa drugs. Trying to clean them all up, you know, as best they could with alcohol. And they also did-- they used DDT on everybody. All of us were dusted with DDT. I mean, we had-- all of us had to-- we had to have our-- our heads, our shirts, our inside of our trousers, inside of our shoes, and our boots. My airplanes were dusted. I mean, they dusted everything because of the-- the typhus, which was in every camp that I was in. There was, uh, a lot of typhus. So there were parasites or fleas, um, that, um, spread typhus.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as a precaution, they just dusted everybody. Every time you went in, you got dusted again. If you went in twice, uh, in one day, you got dusted twice. Uh-- How long were you in Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three days, three and a half days, maybe. And remember, I was the-- the party of these officers from SHAEF were with these other officers. And what they were doing was assessing. They very seldom conferred with us. With-- We were pretty much on our own, except at-- at billet time. We would go back and-- and leave the camp at night, and go to a house where we were billeted, a German house where the families had been evicted, and had our own nice rooms. They just kicked the people out. I mean, this is what they did, SHAEF. Eisenhower said, when you're in Germany, you guys sleep in beds. So I never slept in Germany, I never slept anyplace except in a nice house on a nice bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was interested in the crematoria. You said there were bodies in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, uh, well, this-- this they didn't disturb, except to turn off the fuel. There was no gas chamber in-- in Buchenwald, to my knowledge. The driver, and he and I got to be pretty good buddies. Some of the people that were-- the inmates by now had been told-- they had an organization, by the way, in-- in the camps of prisoners. They were pretty well organized. And um, they had-- they were very, uh, there were some collaborators that were killed, uh, by the inmates. And-- and uh our troops let it happen. There were several instances of where the inmates pointed out former SS guards who had changed into, uh, the uniforms of the inmates or raggedy ass clothes. And what-- the way they handled this was they took him into a cell, into the-- the prison section. And they made a noose of-- of rope and gave it to the inmate-- to the SS person, told him what to do. Fix-- fix-- stand up on the table, fix the rope to the-- to the lamp, or whatever it was that-- that was strong enough, and-- and put it around your neck. And if you do it right, you won't strangle, and step off the table. The table would be maybe this high. Now I didn't see that myself. This guy told me they had done this three or four times. And these were to the SS guards who had tried to stay in the camp as uh, as, inmates. But the inmates knew who they were. And-- and they handled it. And the Americans-- and there's some of this stuff is on the internet. Uh, one of the stories that I submitted was by a fellow named-- uh, well, I'll give it to you. It's on the Cybrary of the Holocaust, if you've ever visited that site. Uh, it tells Harry Herder was a liberator of Buchenwald. And he-- he tells about witnessing this thing. I don't know Harry Herder, but I heard the same story from this guy that was with me. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did you see personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Personally, the-- the-- we went through some of the barracks. There were-- there were some Americans-- POWs in the camp. Not a lot, one or two. Uh, and these were the bad-- these were guys who were incor-- considered incorrigible. And they just threw them in there so they didn't have to fool with them at the stalags and at the POW camps. There were a couple of British guys in there. uh, there were several college professors who spoke English just like we do. And, uh, we got to talk to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Matter of fact, one guy was a rabbi, and he would sit with us and gather around with cigarettes. We'd all have cigarettes and maybe a bottle of brandy or something. And we'd sit in a circle and-- and he would answer questions. He'd been there for six or seven years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you ask him?

speaker: interviewee
text: About the treatment, about the food. The food, uh, in Buchenwald, near the end of the life of most of the camps, it was-- they didn't get any food. They-- Buchenwald was not the worst, but it was terrible. There were-- there were unrecovered dead bodies all over the place. If you go into a row-- between a row of barracks, andw what-- and there were dead bodies. You had to step over or around-- or around dead bodies. Usually they were naked. And what had happened, they were people that had died during the night, and the other inmates would just take them outside. And they had a detail of inmates whose job was to go to take these-- gather up these bodies, and put them in the stacks. Uh, and you know, like I say, the-- I don't know how to describe this. It just was surreal. To me, seeing, uh, a bunch of dead bodies, after a while, it didn't-- it didn't have any impact. It was just was so terrible. I never felt like crying at Buchenwald, uh, except when I was in the one barracks where these people had-- had been. And they were all dying. Some of them were already dead. But their fecal matter was just all over. You walked in it this deep just to get inside the barracks. And uh, And then you realized that these-- these-- they could look at you. They couldn't talk. And of course-- and-- and we were told, you know, like I said, these are-- these-- these guys were on their way out, the way he put it. And the smell was-- it just permeated your pores. You could just, and I had a flat leather flight jacket on, like that one in that picture. Uh, and it just went-- you know-- uh, it didn't make you sick. I never did get nauseated. I got very angry. I became very angry. Uh, I-- I-- [SIGHS] and I'll tell you some more about that later. I-- OK, we-- we saw all the bodies. We saw interviewed, some of these-- or met with some of these, this one rabbi, and some of these college guys, professors who spoke English.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the rabbi say as far as how-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh he was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --was treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very lucid. He was very thin, uh, emaciated. Uh, but he survived. These colleagues, they had-- they had-- they took care of each other. Uh, they had an organization and they would-- they took care of the weak ones and the important ones. The other inmates made sacrifices. If a person had typhus and they knew he was going to die, or they felt he was going to die, they made a deal with him just to take his rations and give it to somebody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever look into the kitchen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I went through the whole thing. And it was-- And it was a typical army type mess hall, but there was no food there in-- in Buchenwald. I mean, you know, there was some bread and some big vats of-- what soup. I suppose you could call it that. But there were turnips. And-- And, um, I never saw any meat at Buchenwald. And we went through the warehouses, too. But they had stripped-- you see, the SS had split. They left Buchenwald, pr-- tried to get away. And they were mostly captured uh, by the military, by the Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was-- there-- there was water, but not enough water to bathe. You know, there were little tric-- you could get a drink of water out of, if you trusted it. We were told not to drink it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, but there was no-- the toilet facilities were, uh, [SIGHS] well, you wouldn't want to go in there. You wouldn't want to go in to where the men-- where the inmates. And there were women and children in-- in Buchenwald. And some of those children had been born in Buchenwald. There were-- They never knew anything else. Another mistake that we did, and I'm sure you've heard this, was give them goodies, give them candies and stuff like that. Uh, we all had this-- these rations. It's part of your-- It's part of your stuff that you carry with you. In case, you know, you need something, you can have a candy bar or something like that. And, uh, the children weren't so emaciated, although they were raggedy ass, [PAUSES] pathetic little tikes. One boy that, uh, the rabbi had there, and we talked to him. And he interpreted for him. I don't know what country he was from. But, uh, he would-- had been born in the camp, and he was about nine years old. But he looked, you know, like a little old man, all wrinkly and-- and bony. And-- and-- uh, but his eyes were sparkly, and, uh, and he was very inquisitive. He wanted to know about-- he didn't know anything about America. He didn't know anything, except what he had experienced in this camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what language did you speak to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the rabbi was speaking to him. And think it was French, but I'm not sure. The rabbi spoke, you know, several languages. And he was-- thank God for him, because I learned more about what this Holocaust business was about in the first instance, by listening to him explain how the structure of the camp was, how the infrastructure, how they were treated by the SS, how some of the-- how they protected each other from the bad guys, the kapos. Uh, there were criminals in there, who-- very cruel. They were ordered to whip each other. There were-- there were men who would refuse to do that, barracks leaders, who of course, were in some cases beaten to death. And in some cases, the SS just ignored them. And he told us those kind of stories. Uh, and-- and we met with him a couple of times. And we'd sit around, like I said, smoking cigarettes. The guy couldn't get enough cigarettes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the, uh, sanitary facilities--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for the record.

speaker: interviewee
text: There wasn't a-- there wasn't any place in there that was sanitary, anywhere. Except in the-- in the SS, uh, sect-- the SS, uh, section of the camp, where the commandant and his-- his men lived. And this is, remember, the Bitch of Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now the commandant, uh, Els-- her name was Elsie, Ilsa, I have it over there. She was, uh, tried. She's the one that had the lampshades. OK? This-- after-- one of the times-- after I had seen the crematoriums and the bodies, stacked bodies, and, uh, wandered-- driven around the camp, and had to walk in someplace where you couldn't drive. This guy said, you gotta see this. I remember him saying that to me. He said, this is unreal. I don't know what the hell this is all about. And it's called the or-- and he said we call it the organ room. I said what, they've got organs in there, music and stuff? He says, ha-ha, kind of sarcastic. So he drove to this building. And we go into this room and here it is. It's-- it' probably [SIGHS] 300 or 400 feet, it, uh, uh, like a lab with lots of shelves. Dissecting tables like, uh, in a hospital. And hundreds of jars and bottles filled with human organs. Eyes. Heads, complete heads. Internal organs, which I couldn't identify. A heart I could identify, a liver maybe. Kidneys. All kinds of organs. Lots of eyes. [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, And-- and even in all-- the realization-- here again, now this was a little difficult for me, when-- because we wouldn't say anything. Just-- we just went around the room like this. And everywhere you looked, there were these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and this guy said, you know, stop and think about this. These people-- these are-- these are body parts. These are people. These are-- these came from people like us. Were they dead when they did this? Were they alive when they did this? And-- and I couldn't-- I-- I-- I didn't cry, but I couldn't answer him. I just-- just the thought that-- [PAUSES] [SIGHS] Oh, Jesus. Because I didn't-- we didn't know. We didn't know. Do you know anything about who was handling that laboratory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only from reading about it, uh, and discussing it on the internet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, can you tell us what you learned?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, it-- it was an experimentation. Uh, the Nazi doctors, the SS doctors, did experiment with, uh, removing-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know the name of--

words.text: You don't know the name of--

speaker: interviewee
text: --removing a woman's ovaries without any anesthetic, for example. uh, heart surgery without-- you know-- or they would kill them with a shot of air to the heart, or some-- or some petrol, benzene I believe it was. And it would kill you right away. Or they would even experiment with to see how much pain you could tolerate.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about the lamps. What was that about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Well, OK. [SIGHS] Boy, I was glad to get the hell out of there. I went back, though. When we-- when we left the organ room, and then he said, I-- we're going to go see the Bitch's house. That's the way he put it to me. And this was in a separate section of, uh, Buchenwald. And it was kind of like a little subdivision, you know, with flowers, and fences, and nice little bungalows, you know, and a nice house, where-- where Ilsa Koch. K-O-C-H was her name. And her husband had been executed. He was an SS guy. I didn't know this until later. He had been executed for-- not for cruelty to, uh, inmates or anything like that. He was executed for screwing the SS out of money. He was-- he was dipping into the SS money. Putting it in, andlike the stuff they would steal from the Jews, he'd rake off, uh, some of it for he and his wife. And, uh, they caught him, and they-- and they executed him. And I was sure glad to read about that. I never saw him. He was dead by the time we got there. She had been arrested. So we went through her house. And of course, they-- now they had guards all over the place by now. You know, this, the Americans were, don't touch anything. And in her house were two lamps. And they had little signs, little tags on them. And they were-- they were human skin. I know this is controversial. There were three lamps that were there when the camp was liberated. All in her house, all made from human skin. And she picked-- this is the story that's in the books.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and at the time, nobody knew. At the time, the guy said, the guy that was my guide sort of-- sort of-- with the-- said that, uh, these-- the medics have said this is human skin. And I said, you've got to be kidding. And it was tattooed skin. Every one of the lamps was very colorfully tattooed. It was a pattern. Some of them were flowers. You know? But-- and you'd touch them, and it was like touching-- it was like touching leather, you know, like you-- or parchment kind of. Kind of like a parchment, only maybe a little thicker. But there were two of them there. So this guy didn't know much about her, except that they had-- they arrested her. And her husband was just missing and they didn't know where the hell he was. But he had already been executed by the SS, as-- for stealing money from the SS.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, thi is getting there, the tail-- uh, the end of Buchenwald. And we-- and we're going to get stuck here if we're not careful. The only other, uh, thing that comes to mind again, of course, is the terrible, uh, odors. The cleanest place in the whole camp was, uh, the houses. And we went through a couple houses, but hers was the only one that had any-- Oh, there were some shrunken heads too, in the lab. But-- I mean, they were in a, they were about this big, and they were in a jar, like a gallon jar, a jug, a gallon jug, with alcohol. And they had long hair and beards. I don't know what the hell they were, but they were human heads.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I've read about them since. And there's a lot of controversy about what these-- some of the deniers say that these were borrowed from a museum. Well, you know, how do they know where they came from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, that's one of the things I love to do is argue with revisionists and deniers on the internet. Because, you know, it's pretty hard to shoot a guy down, when he-- when I'm dealing with a 35-year-old man who only has read books, and-- anti-Holocaust books, anti-Semitic books, pro-Nazi books. As opposed to someone who has not only been there, but now I have about a hundred books that I have read. And I've read every book in the library, the Eugene library, about the Holocaust that they have, since 1992.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now I'm-- I feel qualified to debate with, and I challenged. I don't mean this to sound to be bragging. I have challenged Holocaust deniers to publicly debate me on television. And nobody has accepted the bait. Right here in Eugene. I mean, I'd kick ass and they know it, if you'll pardon the expression. My army slang is coming back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, that-- OK, I went back to the-- to the organ room, and went through that same emotional feeling that I had before. That this is just-- the bodies out there were not as-- [PAUSES] as depressing to me, as the fact that these had been living human beings that had been experimented with one way or another. And of course, it turned out to be that's what this was all about. You know, you know, that's what they were doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I just walked out of there, out-- out of the room. And I-- and I talked to the rabbi about it. He-- he validated what I had suspected. And he said, um, there were horrendous things happened in this place over the years that I've been here. That-- He said, uh, you know, it's hard to believe that people can do things like that to other people. Uh, perform surgery on women. He mentioned ovaries, removal of women's ovaries without any anesthetic. Um, uh, he mentioned, um, experiments with heart surgery, on hearts.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know what they were trying to do. But, uh, there were human hearts in these things. I don't know what they were trying to do. Who knows? I've read about this, but I don't-- I still don't understand it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We left after, I want to emphasize one more time, the importance of, if I hadn't had a chance to sit with that rabbi, and smoke cigarettes and drink-- and I didn't drink. I was only 20 years old. I didn't turn 21 until June the 30th. So I would sip on the brandy or the cognac, and I smoked Lucky Strikes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this guy, I couldn't, I mean I was giving him Lucky Strikes by the cartons. And of course, he was sharing them with-- But he was-- he would smoke 20 cigarettes in 15 minutes. It's really awesome. He would drag on that cigarette, and the fil-- burn that much each time. You know? I mean, I can't do that. I quit smoking. But this-- this was-- thi was awesome.

speaker: interviewee
text: Without him-- without his, uh, availability to me, and to the men that were with me, which were some of the other pilots, and some of the other men who were from the government, military government-- I don't think I would have gone, left Buchenwald with any kind of an understanding that would have prepared me for the next one, which was even worse. [PAUSES] He explained the system to me and how it worked inside the camps. What-- how-- how they treated him as a-- as a-- as a rabbi. Uh, he was more humili-- f you were a Jew, it was bad enough. If you were a rabbi, it was 10 times. They played with them like cats and mice. They made him do things that humans can't do. They had torturing devices, that-- that I would have-- if anything-- if they had used any of those things on me, I would just have told them any damn thing they wanted to know without even thinking about it. Just by looking at these things, the pain must have been-- if they-- And there were people that would not talk. That they just died. And the rabbi told me about it. Trying to get you to tell on somebody who they suspected was planning a getaway, or planning to escape, or maybe had some extra rations, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop this tape now. Did you see any---
